skill.name: npm-release
skill.description: Guides maintainers through the cc-devflow npm release process, including version updates, changelog entries, git tagging, and npm publishing checks.
skill.summary: Standardizes npm release steps for the cc-devflow package.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command examples and hardcoded URLs in SKILL.md. Review found these are release workflow instructions rather than hidden executable code, but they can publish packages, push tags, change remotes, and unpublish npm versions, so the skill should be published with an operational warning. The weak cryptography alerts at SKILL.md lines 3 and 70 are false positives; no cryptographic API or hashing logic is present.

medium_findings.title: High-impact release commands require operator control
medium_findings.description: The skill documents git and npm commands that can push commits and tags, publish cc-devflow to npm, install the latest package globally, unpublish a version, delete a remote tag, and revert commits. These commands match the stated release purpose, but incorrect use could alter public package and repository state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and operational, but they are visible workflow instructions rather than concealed execution logic. The risk is misuse or over-automation of legitimate release commands.

medium_findings.title: Network-dependent release workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow relies on GitHub, npm, GitHub CLI, curl, and SSH connectivity for release creation and verification. These network operations are expected for publishing, but users should verify repository and registry targets before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced URLs and network commands point to GitHub and npm release operations consistent with the skill description. No evidence of data exfiltration or unrelated external endpoints was found.

low_findings.title: Static weak cryptography findings are false positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at the metadata description and a changelog text placeholder. These lines do not invoke cryptographic functions, select hash algorithms, or process secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows ordinary prose, not executable cryptography. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak algorithm use in the skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs reference Semantic Versioning, the cc-devflow changelog, GitHub release pages, GitHub connectivity checks, SSH key settings, and GitHub tags. They are expected documentation or verification targets for this workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and related to versioning, GitHub, or release verification. I found no unrelated collection endpoint or secret-bearing request.

dangerous_patterns.title: External command examples
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md contains many shell examples for git, npm, gh, curl, ssh, and open. They are not executed by the skill file itself, but agents following the skill may run them with repository or registry privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static command detections are real command snippets, and their intent is clear from surrounding release workflow headings. Their risk depends on whether an agent executes them without user confirmation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command substitution in commit and tag messages
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses shell command substitution to build multi-line git commit and tag messages from here-documents. This is a legitimate formatting technique in the documented examples, but substituted shell content should not include untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitution syntax is present and semantically understood. The example uses static here-document text, so I did not find evidence of active command injection.

content.value_statement: Manual npm releases can drift across package.json, changelog entries, git tags, and registry state. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured release checklist for cc-devflow.
actual_capabilities: Checks the expected clean main branch state before release work begins.
Guides semantic version selection for patch, minor, and major releases.
Defines changelog, package.json, commit, and annotated tag update steps.
Includes dry-run and post-publish verification commands for npm.
Documents rollback steps for npm unpublish, tag deletion, and commit revert.
limitations: The workflow is specific to the cc-devflow npm package.
Pre-release and beta version handling requires adaptation.
It does not automate credential management or npm authentication.
It assumes the user has permission to publish and push repository tags.

use_cases.title: Prepare a patch release
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to update version files, changelog notes, and release tags for a bug fix.

use_cases.title: Verify a release before publishing
use_cases.description: Run the pre-release checks and npm dry run before pushing public release artifacts.

use_cases.title: Recover from a broken publish
use_cases.description: Follow rollback guidance when a recently published cc-devflow version has a critical problem.

prompt_templates.title: Check release readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the npm-release skill to check whether this cc-devflow repository is ready for a patch release. Report blockers before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a version bump
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the npm-release skill to decide whether the next cc-devflow release should be patch, minor, or major based on the current changes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare release files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the npm-release skill to update package.json and CHANGELOG.md for version X.Y.Z. Do not publish until I approve.

prompt_templates.title: Complete and verify publish
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the npm-release skill to create the release commit and tag, run npm publish dry-run, then ask before pushing or publishing.

output_examples.input: Check if a release can start.
output_examples.output: A readiness report listing branch status, working tree status, current version, recent commits, and any release blockers.
A concise release plan with the new version, changelog entry outline, package version update, commit message, and tag message.
A verification summary comparing the GitHub tag, npm package version, npm publish time, and installed command version.

best_practices: Run the dry-run publish step before any real npm publish command.
Confirm package.json, CHANGELOG.md, and git tag versions match before pushing.
Ask for explicit approval before commands that push, publish, unpublish, or delete tags.
anti_patterns: Do not use this workflow from a dirty working tree.
Do not publish from a branch other than main without adapting the process.
Do not use rollback commands without confirming the exact affected version.

faq.question: Does this skill publish automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and checkpoints. Users should approve public push and publish actions.

faq.question: Which package is this designed for?
faq.answer: It is written for the cc-devflow npm package and its GitHub repository.

faq.question: Can it handle beta releases?
faq.answer: Not directly. The skill says pre-release and beta versions need adaptation.

faq.question: Why does it include network commands?
faq.answer: Release publishing needs GitHub and npm verification. The URLs are visible workflow targets.

faq.question: What should happen before npm publish?
faq.answer: Confirm a clean main branch, synchronized version markers, a release commit, an annotated tag, and a successful dry run.

faq.question: What is the main security caution?
faq.answer: The commands can change public repository and npm state, so privileged actions need explicit operator approval.
